title: Listera convallarioides
taxonomy: Listera convallarioides is a species of orchid found in North America. It belongs to the family Orchidaceae, which is a large and diverse group of flowering plants. The genus Listera is a group of terrestrial orchids commonly known as twayblades. These plants are typically characterized by their small, often greenish or yellowish flowers and heart-shaped leaves. Listera convallarioides is native to deciduous and mixed forests in eastern North America.
uses: Listera convallarioides, also known as the American orchid, has been used in traditional medicine for various purposes. Its roots are believed to have anti-inflammatory properties and have been used to treat conditions such as fever and rheumatism. In some cultures, the plant is also used to treat respiratory issues like bronchitis and coughs. Additionally, Listera convallarioides is thought to have antimicrobial properties, which may help to reduce the severity of infections. More research is needed to fully understand the medicinal uses and potential benefits of this plant.

side_effects: Listera convallarioides is a type of orchid that contains a toxic compound called listerine. Ingestion of Listera convallarioides can cause nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea. The toxic compound can also lead to more severe symptoms such as abdominal pain and headaches. Listera convallarioides is also known to cause cardiac problems in some individuals. If ingested, it is essential to seek medical attention immediately.
intro: Listera convallarioides, also known as the yellow adder's mouth or bog orchid, is a type of terrestrial orchid that grows in shaded, moist areas. This plant has been used in traditional medicine for its potential health benefits, including its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which may help to soothe digestive issues and promote wound healing. The bioactive constituents of Listera convallarioides include alkaloids, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which are believed to contribute to its medicinal properties.
